summary: Spolsky describes his Seattle demo event and introduces a framework for understanding software development as three distinct phases: design, development, and debugging. Each phase requires fundamentally different scheduling approaches—design is art that's hard to estimate for new projects, development is engineering that can be scheduled precisely with good specs, and debugging is science where prediction is nearly impossible. He argues that skipping the design/spec phase doesn't save time but actually slows you down, since designing on paper is roughly 10x faster than designing in code. The post also promotes FogBugz 6.0's new Evidence-based scheduling feature using Monte Carlo simulation.
key_points: Software development has three phases—design (art), development (engineering), and debugging (science)—regardless of methodology, and each requires fundamentally different scheduling approaches
Development is the only phase that can be scheduled with precision, but only when starting from a detailed specification or blueprint
Bug-finding rates are too noisy to extrapolate shipping dates from, since they reflect testing capacity and tester pool size more than code quality
Skipping specs and designing while coding is like driving with the handbrake on—designing on paper is 10x faster than designing in code, so iterating on specs lets your design improve 10x faster
For brand-new projects, the design phase genuinely cannot be estimated, and that's acceptable
money_quotes: Software development is a cycle with three distinct phases: design, development, and debugging.
Can you imagine Safeco Field filled to the brim with software developers? And that's just the Vista Shutdown Menu Team.
Design is the art phase, where you're doing new, creative work.
Debugging is the science phase. Science is difficult to schedule because you're looking for things, and predicting when you're going to find them is remarkably difficult.
Unless you know in advance how many bugs you're going to find, you don't have an ice cube's chance in the Sahara to work out a detailed estimate of how long this phase will take.
Designing a feature by writing a thoughtful spec takes about 1/10th as much time as writing the code for that feature—or less.
If you ever started a new project by writing code, and you thought you'd 'design as you went along,' what you're doing is driving around with the handbrakes on.
Your product's design can only get better at 1/10th the speed that my product's design can.
key_insight: Each phase of software development—design, development, and debugging—is fundamentally different in nature and requires its own scheduling approach, and trying to skip the design phase by coding first makes your design iterate 10x slower.
